text: In this particular case, it was apparent from the outset that the tumour was adhered to the aorta as a result of tumour spread.
Here we are dissecting into the sub-inventitial plane to ensure removal of any diseased mediastinal pleura.

Here we're clipping and dividing the superior intercostal vein, and it's worth mentioning at this point that whilst care would usually be taken to protect the vagus, that in this particular case it was necessary to divide it.

Next you can see us examining the posterior hilum here, just using a blunt instrument to reflect the pleura to obtain a view of whether there's involvement of the left bronchus, pulmonary artery, and more superiorly the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
Next we turn our attention to the bronchus.
You can see us here using the diathermy on the end of shears to remove the pleural overlying.
The idea here is to scoliginise the bronchus using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection.
To the right of the bronchus here the station 5 lymph nodes come into view, with the station 7 lymph node just to the left of the bronchus as shown here.
In this particular case, the station 7 node was sent for frozen section to ensure there was no metastatic involvement of the N2 lymph nodes.
The return pathology was negative for disease and another benefit of removing the node at this stage is the resultant clearance of the subcarinal space.
Next, the right angled Roberts is passed anterior to the bronchus with care being taken not to damage the pulmonary artery.
A vascular sling is then placed into the jaws of the Roberts which can then be used to retract
providing better views of the proximal bronchus.
This manoeuvre also allows better visualisation of the station 5 lymph nodes which are removed here with the node holding forceps.
Attention is then turned to the inferior and anterior hilum where a combination of blunt and sharp dissection is used here to dissect the inferior pulmonary ligament
Anteriorly, the pleura is taken posterior to the phrenic nerve, which facilitates assessment of the pulmonary vein, which in this case was free from disease.

Following this assessment, the decision to proceed to pneumonectomy can now be made, and Roberts is passed behind the superior pulmonary vein here, which is now taken with a TAN45 tri-stapler, passed through the posterior port, and then fired.
Attention is now turned to the bronchus which is retracted with a vascular sling to enable taking of the bronchus as close to the carina as possible.
Care must be taken here to ensure the pulmonary artery is not caught which is lying directly anterior to the bronchus at this stage.
Stapler is passed through the anterior port and fired.
Following division of the bronchus, clear views are allowed of the pulmonary artery and you can see the Roberts being passed behind it here to create a passageway for the TAN45 tri stapler.
The stapler is passed through the anterior utility port and the aim here is to ensure the stapler is flush with the pulmonary artery prior to firing.
This is accomplished with some gentle rotation back and forth as demonstrated here.
Next the stapler is closed and then fired.
The final vest to be taken is the inferior pulmonary vein.
In this particular case this was left to last to prevent vascular engorgement which can often hinder progress.
You can see here the Tan45 tri stapler being manoeuvred into position, just past either side of the vein.
Some gentle manoeuvring, closed and then fired.
This step completes the vision of the hyalur structures.
Following this haemostasis is achieved.
prior to removal of the specimen through a plastic bag as shown here to prevent contamination and seeding in the wound.
Following this manoeuvre, the bronchus is checked for air leaks, a paravertebral catheter is placed adjacent to the sympathetic chain and a chest chain is placed.
